data.title: Strait of Hormuz reopens as U.S.-Iran peace talks progress
data.description:
Investing.com - Iran announced Friday it would reopen the Strait of Hormuz for the duration of a 10-day ceasefire between Israel and Hezbollah in Lebanon. [[pro_promotion |
Unlock the hottest news by upgrading to InvestingPro - get 50% off today
]]
The waterway for global energy supplies, which has been shut since the U.S. and Israel started bombing Iran at the end of February, is now "completely open" for commercial shipping, Iran’s Foreign Minister Abbas Araghchi said in a post on X.
President Donald Trump responded on X, saying "IRAN HAS JUST ANNOUNCED THAT THE STRAIT OF IRAN IS FULLY OPEN AND READY FOR FULL PASSAGE. THANK YOU!"
Trump added that "the Strait of Hormuz is completely open and ready for business and full passage, but the naval blockade will remain in full force and effect as it pertains to Iran, only, until such time as our transaction with Iran is 100% complete. This process should go very quickly in that most of the points are already negotiated."
Trump said in a phone interview with Bloomberg on Friday that Iran agreed to suspend its nuclear program indefinitely and will not receive any frozen funds from the U.S. The president said a deal to end the war, which the U.S. and Israel began with Iran in late February, is mostly complete.
Trump told Axios he expects an Iran deal "in a day or two" and said talks over a lasting agreement will "probably" be held this weekend. The discussions are expected to take place in Islamabad on Sunday, according to a source familiar with the mediation efforts. Pakistan is mediating the negotiations with support from Egypt and Turkey.
Iran has yet to comment on any deal beyond the Hormuz opening, nor on claims made by Trump on Thursday that Tehran had offered concessions, including over the key issue of its nuclear program.
Trump said he hasn’t decided who would lead a U.S. delegation for talks with Iranian officials to sign an agreement. Asked if he would travel to Pakistan, which hosted the last round of negotiations, the president said, "I may."
JD Vance led discussions with Iranian officials last weekend, and Trump said he was considering sending the vice president alongside his son-in-law, Jared Kushner, and envoy Steve Witkoff for the talks.

data.title: Trump threatens to bomb Iran again if no deal is reached by Wednesday
data.description:
Investing.com - President Donald Trump has issued a stark ultimatum regarding the ongoing Middle East conflict, warning that he may decline to extend a ceasefire with Iran if a final agreement is not reached by next week Wednesday.
[[pro_promotion |
Track energy and defense stocks with InvestingPro — now 50% off
]]
Ultimatum on ceasefire
Speaking to reporters aboard Air Force One, Trump suggested that the window for a diplomatic resolution is closing rapidly. 
When asked whether he would extend the temporary truce that expires this coming Wednesday, the President replied, "Maybe I won’t extend it… and we’ll have to start dropping bombs again." 
Trump also hinted at the necessity of seizing Iran’s enriched uranium, stating, "We’ll go in with Iran, and we will take it together, and we will bring it back to the US. If we don’t do that, we will get it in a different form, a much more unfriendly form."
The comments come amid persistent skepticism from both Iranian officials and the shipping industry. 
Despite Trump’s earlier claims of a breakthrough regarding the Strait of Hormuz, a senior Iranian official described the demand to ship the nation’s enriched uranium stockpile to the U.S. as a "non-starter."
Market skepticism persists
Financial markets and energy analysts remain on edge as the rhetoric intensifies. Data from Kpler indicates that despite optimistic headlines regarding the reopening of the Strait of Hormuz, real-world shipping activity remains severely constrained. 
Many commercial vessels have been observed abruptly turning away from the waterway and deactivating their tracking systems, suggesting that shippers still view the route as effectively closed.
Trump maintained in subsequent remarks that "I think it’s gonna happen", referring to a final deal. The disconnect between his administration’s public optimism and the logistical reality on the ground has left many observers questioning the stability of the current negotiations. 
As the mid-week deadline approaches, the threat of renewed military action continues to loom over global energy markets already grappling with severe supply chain volatility.

data.title: Fifth Third shares fall over 2% on merger costs despite earnings beat
data.description: CINCINNATI - On Friday, Fifth Third Bancorp (NASDAQ:FITB) reported first quarter earnings that exceeded analyst expectations.The bank’s shares fell 2.38% in pre-market trading after the release as investors digested substantial merger-related expenses from the recently completed Comerica acquisition.The Cincinnati-based bank posted adjusted earnings per share of $0.15, beating the analyst consensus estimate of -$0.10. However, reported results included a net negative impact of $0.68 per share from merger-related charges and other items. Revenue reached $2.83 billion on a fully taxable-equivalent basis, up 33% YoY, driven primarily by the Comerica acquisition that closed February 1st.Net interest income rose 34% YoY to $1.94 billion, while net interest margin expanded 27 basis points to 3.30% compared to the prior year. The Comerica transaction added $86 billion in assets, $51 billion in loans, and $65 billion in deposits at closing. Average portfolio loans increased 30% YoY to $158 billion, and average deposits climbed 28% to $209 billion."The first quarter reflected continued momentum across Fifth Third," said CEO Tim Spence. "We delivered strong loan and deposit growth, driven by new commercial relationships and continued household expansion. We closed the acquisition of Comerica on February 1st, and early financial benefits are already showing up, including strong net interest margin expansion and tangible book value per share growth."Credit quality remained solid, with net charge-offs of 37 basis points, the lowest level since the fourth quarter of 2023. The nonperforming asset ratio improved to 0.57% from 0.81% a year earlier.Noninterest expense totaled $2.40 billion, up 84% YoY, reflecting $635 million in pre-tax merger-related charges during the quarter, representing approximately half of expected full-year integration costs. Excluding certain items, adjusted noninterest expense was $1.77 billion.The CET1 capital ratio stood at 9.96%, down from 10.43% in the prior year, primarily reflecting capital impacts from the Comerica acquisition.This article was generated with the support of AI and reviewed by an editor. For more information see our T&C.

data.title: Anthropic in talks with EU Commission on AI models
data.description: Investing.com - U.S. artificial intelligence company Anthropic is in discussions with the European Commission regarding its various models, including cybersecurity offerings that have not yet launched in the EU, the Commission said Friday.Commission spokesman Thomas Regnier told reporters in Brussels that Anthropic has committed to comply with the European Union’s general purpose artificial intelligence code of practice.Under this framework, companies must assess and mitigate potential risks from services that may or may not be offered in Europe, Regnier said.The discussions come as the EU works to regulate AI technologies through its code of practice, which requires companies to evaluate risks associated with their AI systems before deployment in European markets.This article was generated with the support of AI and reviewed by an editor. For more information see our T&C.

data.title: Hedge fund stock buying hits $86 billion as Iran peace hopes, Goldman data shows
data.description: By Nell Mackenzie LONDON, April 17 (Reuters) - Systematic hedge funds boughts stock at a record pace in the last five trading sessions, adding $86 billion of stock exposure, said a Goldman Sachs note published late Thursday and seen by Reuters on Friday shows. Such hedge funds use algorithms to ride market trends until they peter out and use market signals rather than views on the economic aspects of the company stocks they trade. World stocks held near record highs on Friday, set for their a third straight week of gains, as investors awaited a crucial weekend that could pave the way for a near-term resolution to the Middle East war.  Key points from the Goldman Sachs data: • The last five sessions have garnered one of "the largest in history" trading demand from CTAs (Commodity Trading Advisors), or hedge funds that ride market trends. • Since markets turned around at start of April, hedge funds - particularly those that trade systematically - have been equity buyers, betting that asset values would rise. • The rate at which speculators have bought global equities over the last week is in the top five buying speeds of all time. • Goldman Sachs calculations estimate that these speculators could continue to buy stocks and purchase an additional $70 billion of the next five sessions. • The last time global CTAs bought stocks with this fervor was in August 2024, November 2023 and September 2019.

data.title: Jefferies says oil pullback eases Canada rate-hike bets
data.description: Investing.com - Market expectations for Bank of Canada rate increases have eased to 1.2 hikes from 1.6 just last week following a pullback in oil prices after a ceasefire announcement, according to Jefferies. The firm said elevated oil prices are beginning to filter through to the cost of living, including groceries.The Bank of Canada faces pressure from higher living costs, weak labour markets and CUSMA uncertainty, Jefferies said. Canada is willing to address U.S. concerns to achieve a comprehensive trade deal, the firm noted.Canadian banks are seeing productivity gains from AI usage, Jefferies said. Housing markets are split, with price drops in Toronto and Vancouver and strong growth in smaller cities.Statistics Canada is releasing housing starts data for the month of March today. The data comes as the country’s housing market shows divergent trends across regions.Jefferies said dark clouds are hovering over the Canadian economy amid weak labour markets and looming CUSMA renegotiations.This article was generated with the support of AI and reviewed by an editor. For more information see our T&C.

data.title: Truist shares edge down on revenue shortfall despite earnings beat
data.description: CHARLOTTE - On Friday, Truist Financial Corp. (NYSE:TFC) reported first quarter earnings that exceeded analyst expectations, though revenue fell slightly short of estimates.The company’s shares edged down 1.58% in pre-market trading following the results.The bank posted adjusted earnings per share of $1.09, beating the consensus estimate of $1.00 by $0.09, or 9%. This represented a 25% increase from $0.87 per share in the first quarter of 2025. However, revenue of $5.15 billion missed the $5.17 billion analyst estimate and declined 5.1% YoY from $4.90 billion.Net interest income on a taxable-equivalent basis decreased 2.8% to $3.64 billion compared to the fourth quarter of 2025, driven by two fewer days and deposit client mix. The net interest margin contracted five basis points to 3.02%. Compared to the first quarter of 2025, net interest income increased 2.5%, supported by fixed-rate asset repricing and loan growth.Noninterest income remained stable at $1.55 billion, as higher investment banking and trading income offset lower other income. Noninterest expense declined $187 million, or 5.9%, to $2.98 billion, primarily due to lower other expense, personnel expense, and professional fees."We delivered a strong first quarter, with earnings per share up 25% from the first quarter of 2025, driven by disciplined execution against our strategic priorities and continued momentum across the franchise," said Bill Rogers, Truist Chairman & CEO.Average loans held for investment increased $2.3 billion, or 0.7%, to $327.0 billion, driven by commercial portfolio growth. Average deposits rose $2.9 billion, or 0.7%, to $398.9 billion.The provision for credit losses totaled $479 million, down from $512 million in the fourth quarter. Net charge-offs increased to 0.61% of average loans from 0.57% in the prior quarter. The allowance for loan and lease losses remained flat at 1.53% of loans.Truist maintained a Common Equity Tier 1 ratio of 10.8% and returned $1.8 billion to shareholders through dividends and share repurchases during the quarter.This article was generated with the support of AI and reviewed by an editor. For more information see our T&C.

data.title: Bernstein sees a buying opportunity in this major chip stock
data.description: Bernstein sees a buying opportunity in a leading semiconductor equipment maker, pointing to stronger growth prospects in the second half of 2026 and into 2027 despite near-term softness in guidance.The broker highlighted ASML Holding NV after the company delivered first-quarter results that exceeded expectations on revenue, margins and earnings, even as weaker-than-expected second-quarter guidance weighed on sentiment.The Dutch chip equipment giant, the world’s sole supplier of extreme ultraviolet lithography machines, reported Q1 2026 revenue of €8.8 billion, ahead of both consensus and Bernstein estimates of €8.7 billion, while earnings per share came in at €7.15, roughly 8% above street expectations. Gross margin was 53%, at the high end of guidance and above consensus forecasts of 52%.The softer outlook for the current quarter drove the cautious near-term reaction. The company guided Q2 sales to a range of €8.4 billion to €9 billion, below Bernstein’s estimate of €9.5 billion and consensus of €9.1 billion. Gross margin for the quarter is expected at a midpoint of 51%, compared with consensus expectations of 52.3%.Despite this, Bernstein raised its 2027 forecasts, increasing its EPS estimate to €46.98 from €42.60 and revenue to €50.1 billion from €46.7 billion, around 20.1% above consensus EPS of €39.12. The broker maintained its “outperform” rating and left its price target unchanged at €1,700, based on a revised price-to-earnings multiple of 35x, down from 40x.The upgrade in long-term expectations is driven by a stronger outlook for extreme ultraviolet (EUV) systems. Bernstein now expects low-NA EUV capacity to reach at least 80 units in 2027, representing 33% year-on-year growth. A shift in product mix toward higher-throughput systems such as the NXE:3800E and the newer NXE:3800F is expected to lift average selling prices by about 11%.As a result, EUV revenue is forecast to grow 46% year-on-year in 2027. Deep ultraviolet (DUV) revenue is expected to increase 17%, based on an assumption that China revenue remains flat, a scenario Bernstein described as conservative, while rest-of-world DUV demand grows around 30%.For full-year 2026, the company raised its revenue guidance to a range of €36 billion to €40 billion, up from €34 billion to €39 billion previously, implying a midpoint of €38 billion broadly in line with consensus estimates of €38.1 billion. Gross margin guidance was unchanged at 51% to 53%.Regional and segment trends in the first quarter showed strong demand from South Korea, which accounted for 45% of total system sales, the highest level on record, with memory customers contributing 51% of revenue. China’s share declined to 19%, the lowest since the first quarter of 2023.EUV revenue reached a record €4.14 billion during the quarter, with 16 systems shipped, including two High-NA units.Bernstein analysts expect revenue to grow at a compound annual rate of around 15% between 2025 and 2030, reaching €64.8 billion, with gross margins expanding to 59.4% by 2030 from 52.7% in 2026, underpinned by continued mix improvement and scaling of advanced lithography systems.

data.title: Netflix shares fall 10% on Q2 guidance miss; co-founder Hastings to exit
data.description:
Investing.com - Netflix forecasted second-quarter (Q2) profit below Wall Street estimates, sending its shares tumbling nearly 10% in premarket trading Friday.
Netflix sees Q2 2026 earnings per share of $0.78, versus the Wall Street consensus of $0.84. Netflix sees Q2 2026 revenue of $12.57 billion, which was also below the estimates of $12.64 billion.
In a letter to investors released on ​Thursday, Netflix also said Chairman Reed Hastings will not stand for re-election at ⁠its annual meeting in June and will be leaving the streaming service he co-founded 29 years ago as he plans to focus on philanthropy and ​other pursuits.
[[pro_promotion |
Get more insight on the market by upgrading to InvestingPro at 50% off ]]
"Growth in content amortization will be first-half weighted due to the timing of title launches. We expect Q2 to have the highest year-over-year content amortization growth rate in 2026, before decelerating to mid-to-high single digit growth in the second half of the year," the company said. 
The streaming giant, however, reported first-quarter (Q1) profit that sailed past analyst estimates as subscription revenue remained strong.
It reported Q1 earnings per share of $1.23, $0.44 better than the analyst estimate of $0.79. Its revenue for the quarter came in at $12.25 billion that jumped 16.2% from the year-ago period and also beat the consensus estimate of $12.18 billion.
“Revenue in Q1 grew 16% year over year (+14% on a foreign exchange (F/X) neutral basis), driven primarily by membership growth, higher pricing, and increased ad revenue,” it said in a shareholder letter.
Netflix said it continues to project 2026 revenue of $50.7 billion -$51.7 billion and an operating margin (OM) of 31.5%.  
’With the stock up 40% in the last 2 months (vs. COMP +7%), investors are likely disappointed by the Q2 rev guidance miss and no raise on the FY26 rev/ OM outlook," said Jefferies analyst James Heaney. 
"In our view, the primary issue was overly optimistic expectations for U.S. pricing benefit and margin expectation, rather than any fundamental deterioration," he added. 
The results come after Netflix’ failed to acquire Warner Bros. Discovery for $72 billion and attempts to regain its footing as the largest streamer. Netflix, which ​long told investors ⁠that a Warner Bros acquisition was a "nice to have, not need to have" proposition, highlighted areas of future growth did not tell how it will spend $2.8 billion termination fee it received. 
The ​company said it is expanding its entertainment offerings with ​video ⁠podcasts, and live entertainment - such as the World Baseball Classic in Japan - is fuelling engagement. It plans to leverage technology to improve the user experience and improve ⁠monetization, as ​advertising revenue remains on track to reach $3 ​billion in 2026 - a twofold increase from a year ago. 
Following its callback with management, Raymond James analysts said the team noted that "60% of users in ad markets are now coming in through the ad plan."
"While advertising is not yet a major margin driver, Netflix sees substantial runway through better ad tech, greater use of first-party data, and new GenAI-enabled ad products," the broker added. 
(Jaiveer Shekhawat contributed to this report.)

data.title: Oil prices hover below $100 a barrel on prospects for Middle East peace
data.description:
Investing.com - Oil prices fell on Friday after U.S. President Donald Trump suggested that negotiations between Washington and Tehran may resume this weekend, as a fragile ceasefire between Israel and Lebanon appeared to be holding.
Brent oil futures declined 2.0% to $97.36 a barrel, while U.S. West Texas Intermediate crude futures dropped 2.6% to $92.19 a barrel by 05:24 ET (09:24 GMT).
At these levels, Brent, the global oil benchmark is on track for a gain of over 1% in the past one-week period and WTI for a drop of more than 5%.
[[pro_promotion |Get more oil market insights with InvestingPro - now 50% off]]
Washington and Tehran are “very close” to reaching a deal, Trump said, adding that Iran has agreed not to possess a nuclear weapon for more than 20 years. A desire to quell Iran’s nuclear ambitions has been cited Trump as a central reason for the war, which began with joint U.S. and Israeli strikes on Iran in late February.
In return, Iran has called for the removal of international sanctions.
Trump flagged that he would consider extending the ceasefire if Washington was close to an agreement with Tehran.
Crucially, a new 10-day halt to hostilities between Israel and Lebanon could remove another key sticking point in negotiations. Despite the U.S.-Iran ceasefire, Israel has continued to carry out strikes on Iran-aligned Hezbollah militants in neighboring Lebanon. Iran has demanded that such attacks stop before an agreement with the U.S. can be secured.
“I hope Hezbollah acts nicely and well during this important period of time,” Trump said in a social media post. 
Oil below $100
Oil prices sat below $100 a barrel, with traders keeping tabs on hopes for a long-term peace deal.
Following the outbreak of the war, crude briefly surged to as high as $120 a barrel, compared to pre-conflict levels of around $70 a barrel.
Underpinning much of the surge has been the effective closure of the Strait of Hormuz, a narrow waterway off of Iran’s southern coast through which roughly a fifth of the world’s oil squeezes. Analysts at ING have estimated that around 13 million barrels per day of oil have been disrupted by the shuttering of the strait.
The uptick has in turn sparked fears around a spike in inflation in countries around the world that could dampen global economic growth. There has been subsequent debate around the cascading impact of these trends on everything from central bank interest rate policy to gold and currencies.
Both the International Energy Agency and the Organization of Petroleum Exporting Countries warned of softer demand in the coming months, while a trickle of shipping through the Strait of Hormuz and an ongoing U.S. blockade of Iranian ports may hit supplies.
"Control of the strait remains the main flashpoint,” OCBC analysts said, warning that U.S.-Iran negotiations could take as much as six months.
France and Britain are set to chair a meeting on Friday of roughly 40 countries which aims to signal to the U.S. that they are willing to play a part in unblocking the strait. Trump has frequently criticized other nations, including U.S. allies, for not immediately helping Washington’s efforts to reopen navigation through the chokepoint.
Meanwhile, a U.S. blockade of Iran that began earlier this week has intensified. U.S. military officials have stressed that the restrictions apply to Iran’s ports and coastline, not the Strait of Hormuz.
“It’s unclear if, and how, the U.S. blockade [...] will shift Iran’s negotiating stance, but the regime is clearly now in a worse position than it was when oil exports were flowing,” said Jason Tuvey, Deputy Chief Emerging Markets Economist at Capital Economics, in a note.
(Ambar Warrick contributed reporting)
